input.title: Compile the full-API smoke-test probe at C++20
input.description:
check_binary_symbols.py's check_stable_only_symbols() compiles a probe that includes torch/all.h and ATen/ATen.h, but pins the compile at -std=c++17. Since #178150 ("[12/12] Enforce C++20 minimum in header guards") those two headers hard-error under a pre-C++20 compiler, so the probe now fails to compile with:

  torch/all.h:5:2: error: #error C++20 or later compatible compiler is
  required to use PyTorch.

which trips every manywheel-*-test job (the smoke test aborts on the first failing check). Bump only this probe to -std=c++20 to match the standard the full API now requires.

The stable / header-only / C-shim checks below deliberately stay at -std=c++17: they exercise the portable ABI surface, which does not pull in the C++20-guarded headers and must remain buildable under the older standard, so their flag is left unchanged.

Fixes #190527.

## Test plan

input.title: [inductor] Add barrier before reading back a buffer stored in a reduction loop
input.description:
Fixes #190517

If a kernel stores a buffer and then reads it back in a reduction loop, the
store and the read-back can land on different warps, so a warp may read the
value before another warp's write is visible.

This is the read-after-write side of the write-after-read guard already in
`store()` (triton #1615). `self.cse.invalidated_stores` already tracks the
"stored, then reloaded across a reduction-loop boundary" set, so we add a
`tl.debug_barrier()` before such a load.

pit_b_224 (timm, XPU, AMP fp16 inference) now passes.

input.title: [Test] Make ComposabilityTest backend resolution hardware-agnostic in…
input.description:
## Summary
This PR makes `ComposabilityTest` in `test/distributed/test_composability.py` hardware-agnostic by replacing the hard-coded `"nccl"` backend string with `torch.distributed.get_default_backend_for_device(device_type)`, and by replacing `@requires_nccl()` decorators with `@requires_accelerator_dist_backend()`. The test now adapts to any accelerator supported by `torch.accelerator` (including PrivateUse1-based out-of-tree backends such as NPU) instead of forcing NCCL.

## Changes
Updated `ComposabilityTest` in `test/distributed/test_composability.py`:
- Replaced the hard-coded `return "nccl"` in `ComposabilityTest.backend_str()` with `return torch.distributed.get_default_backend_for_device(device_type)`, so the backend string is resolved dynamically from the framework's device→backend map.
- Replaced the import of `requires_nccl` from `torch.testing._internal.common_distributed` with `requires_accelerator_dist_backend`. Dropped the `requires_nccl` reference entirely.
- Changed the three `@requires_nccl()` decorators on `ComposabilityTest` test methods (lines 201, 282, 393) to `@requires_accelerator_dist_backend()`, so the tests are gated on whether the current accelerator has a registered distributed backend rather than on NCCL specifically.
- Introduced a module-level `device_type` constant derived from `torch.accelerator.current_accelerator(check_available=True)` (falling back to `"cpu"`), used both by `backend_str()` and the `device` property (`torch.device(device_type, self.rank)`) so tensor allocation targets the current accelerator instead of assuming CUDA.

## Motivation
`ComposabilityTest` hard-coded `"nccl"` as the distributed backend and used `@requires_nccl()` to gate execution. On accelerators whose default backend is not NCCL (e.g., NPU uses HCCL, XPU uses XCCL), the test was either skipped or failed at process-group initialization because the framework could not bind NCCL to a non-CUDA device. Using `torch.distributed.get_default_backend_for_device()` and `requires_accelerator_dist_backend()` lets the test pick up the correct backend for any accelerator registered with `torch.accelerator` without introducing a new test class.

## Test Plan
Verified on CPU and across multiple accelerator types:
```
python test/distributed/test_composability.py -v
```
On a PrivateUse1 (NPU) host (8 devices): the three `@requires_accelerator_dist_backend()`-decorated tests execute with `backend_str()` returning `"hccl"` (resolved via `default_device_backend_map["npu"]`), and tensors are allocated on `torch.device("npu", rank)` instead of `cuda`.
On a CPU-only host all three are skipped, because `requires_accelerator_dist_backend()` returns False when no accelerator is available.
On a CUDA host the decorator still runs the tests, with `backend_str()` returning `"nccl"` and tensors allocated on `cuda:rank`, matching the previous behavior.

## Notes
- This change depends on the framework exposing `torch.distributed.get_default_backend_for_device()` (already present in `torch/distributed/distributed_c10d.py`) and `requires_accelerator_dist_backend()` (already present in `torch/testing/_internal/common_distributed.py`).
- The framework's `Backend.default_device_backend_map` must contain an entry for the target accelerator type (e.g., `"npu": "hccl"`). For NPU this is registered by `torch_npu` at import time via `Backend.register_backend()`.
- `device_type` is computed once at module load time. If the test is launched under an environment where `torch.accelerator.current_accelerator()` returns `None` (e.g., CPU-only hosts), it falls back to `"cpu"`, in which case `requires_accelerator_dist_backend()` skips the test before `backend_str()` is ever consulted.
- No framework constant is modified by this PR; the test file only consumes framework APIs.

@wjlFlyer @pjyFight @FuDdd @fffrog @lyriexs666 @jishuangfeng @JamesD18 @JiasenTian

input.title: [inductor] Missing barrier on same-kernel global buffer read-back (RAW) causes accuracy failure
input.description:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

### Summary

When an inductor Triton kernel stores a buffer and then reads it back later in the same kernel, no barrier is emitted between the store and the load. The store and the read-back can end up on different warps, so a warp may read the location before another warp's write is visible - a read-after-write race through global memory.

This is the read-after-write counterpart to the write-after-read guard in `store()` (triton issue #1615). That direction is guarded; store-then-read-back is not.

### Repro
pit_b_224 (timm), inductor + AMP fp16 inference on Intel XPU:

      TORCHINDUCTOR_FORCE_DISABLE_CACHES=1 python benchmarks/dynamo/timm_models.py \
        -d xpu --accuracy --amp --inference --backend=inductor \
        --cold-start-latency -n10 --disable-cudagraphs --only pit_b_224

Result: fail_accuracy (RMSE ~0.4 vs tol ~0.001).

The generated kernel stores `out_ptr0` and reads it back in the reduction loop with nothing in between:

      tl.store(out_ptr0 + (r0_2 + 256*x3), tmp26, r0_mask & xmask)
      ...
      tmp34 = tl.load(out_ptr0 + (r0_2 + 256*x3), r0_mask & xmask, ...)

### Versions

input.title: [linalg] Add 32-bit LAPACK limits check on CPU for SVD, QR, Eig, and Lstsq
input.description:
Add 32-bit LAPACK limits check on CPU for linalg.svd, linalg.qr, linalg.eig, and linalg.lstsq

Fixes #190509

This commit introduces validation checks to prevent integer overflow in 32-bit LAPACK calls on CPU.

Root Cause:
LAPACK dimensions and workspace requirements are represented as 32-bit signed integers. When calling linalg.svd, linalg.qr, linalg.eig, or linalg.lstsq on very large matrices, the required workspace sizes or the matrix dimensions can exceed std::numeric_limits<int>::max(), leading to integer overflow and undefined behavior/crashes.

Fix:
- Added early checking of matrix dimensions (m and n) against INT_MAX on CPU for svd, qr, eig, and lstsq.
- Added early checking of workspace thresholds on CPU for real SVD (min(m, n) < 26755) and complex SVD (min(m, n) < 46340).
- Added early dimension checks in linalg_eig_out before evaluating input.isfinite().all() to prevent allocator errors on large logical views.

Test Plan:
Run PyTorch unit tests:
```bash
PYTHONPATH=test python test/test_linalg.py -k "test_svd_large_matrix_error"
PYTHONPATH=test python test/test_linalg.py -k "test_linalg_ops_dimension_overflow_error"
```

input.title: [Test] Make skip_if_no_gpu hardware-agnostic in common_distributed
input.description:
## Summary
This PR makes `skip_if_no_gpu` hardware-agnostic by replacing its hard-coded CUDA/HPU/XPU checks with `torch.accelerator.*` APIs in `torch/testing/_internal/common_distributed.py`. The decorator name retains "gpu" for backward compatibility, but it now works for any accelerator supported by `torch.accelerator` (including PrivateUse1-based out-of-tree backends such as NPU). The PR also extends the `TEST_SKIPS` table with accelerator-named skip codes so future device-agnostic decorators can emit accelerator-named skip reasons, while keeping the legacy `no_cuda` / `multi-gpu-N` entries unchanged for backward compatibility.

## Changes
Updated `skip_if_no_gpu` in `torch/testing/_internal/common_distributed.py`:
- Commented out the previous implementation that branched on `TEST_CUDA or TEST_HPU or TEST_XPU` and called `torch.cuda.device_count()` / `torch.hpu.device_count()` / `torch.xpu.device_count()` separately, exiting with `TEST_SKIPS["no_cuda"]` when none of those flags was set.
- Introduces a new active `skip_if_no_gpu` that resolves the current accelerator via `torch.accelerator.current_accelerator(check_available=True)`, exits with `TEST_SKIPS["no_accelerator"]` when no accelerator is available, and otherwise obtains the device module through `torch.get_device_module(acc.type)` and compares `dm.device_count()` against `WORLD_SIZE`, exiting with `TEST_SKIPS[f"multi-gpu-{world_size}"]` on shortfall. The "multi-gpu-N" key is reused for backward-compatible exit codes.
- Updates the docstring to drop the `torch.cuda.device(rank)` mention, since device assignment is no longer CUDA-specific.
- Extends the `TEST_SKIPS` table with new entries `no_accelerator` (exit code 89, "accelerator is not available.") and `multi-accelerator-1` through `multi-accelerator-8` (exit codes 90-97, "Need at least N accelerator devices"). Legacy `no_cuda` / `multi-gpu-N` entries are kept untouched to preserve compatibility with any caller that still references them.
- Retains the commented-out previous implementation in the file (wrapped in a `'''...'''` block) for reference; it can be removed in a follow-up cleanup.

The decorator keeps the name `skip_if_no_gpu` so existing call sites (e.g., the 8 tests in `test/distributed/optim/test_zero_redundancy_optimizer.py`) continue to work without modification. No test file is changed by this PR.

## Motivation
`skip_if_no_gpu` only recognized CUDA, HPU, and XPU. New accelerators (e.g., PrivateUse1-based out-of-tree backends like NPU) were silently skipped even when enough devices were available because `TEST_CUDA`, `TEST_HPU`, and `TEST_XPU` are all False for those backends, causing the decorator to exit with `TEST_SKIPS["no_cuda"]`. Using `torch.accelerator` lets distributed tests adapt to any accelerator supported by PyTorch without introducing a new decorator name or modifying existing call sites.

## Test Plan
Verified on CPU and across multiple accelerator types:
```
python test/distributed/optim/test_zero_redundancy_optimizer.py -v
```
On a PrivateUse1 (NPU) host (8 devices): all 8 `@skip_if_no_gpu`-decorated ZeRO tests (`test_step`, `test_step_with_closure`, `test_lr_scheduler`, `test_multiple_param_groups`, `test_collect_shards`, `test_state_dict`, `test_zero_join_gpu`, `test_zero_redundancy_optimizer_eager`) execute instead of being skipped, because `torch.accelerator.current_accelerator()` returns the NPU accelerator and `torch.get_device_module("npu").device_count()` returns 8.
On a CPU-only host all 8 are skipped via `TEST_SKIPS["no_accelerator"]` (exit code 89), matching the previous behavior previously triggered by `TEST_SKIPS["no_cuda"]`.
On a CUDA host the decorator still runs the tests, since CUDA is exposed through `torch.accelerator` and `torch.get_device_module("cuda")` returns the `torch.cuda` module.

## Notes
- This change depends on the framework exposing `torch.accelerator.current_accelerator()` and `torch.get_device_module()` (both already present in `torch/__init__.py`).
- The `TEST_ACCELERATOR` flag in `torch/testing/_internal/common_utils.py` (defined as `LazyVal(lambda: torch.accelerator.is_available())`) is not directly referenced by the new decorator; the decorator instead uses `torch.accelerator.current_accelerator(check_available=True)` so it can both detect availability and obtain the accelerator type in a single call.
- The commented-out previous implementation is intentionally retained in the file for reference; it can be removed in a follow-up cleanup.

@wjlFlyer @pjyFight @FuDdd @fffrog @lyriexs666 @jishuangfeng @JamesD18 @JiasenTian

input.title: [cuda] Remove host sync from masked_fill_ with a device value tensor
input.description:
## Summary

`masked_fill_` with a value that lives on the GPU is slower than it needs to be, and it cannot be captured into a CUDA graph.

The CUDA overload reads the fill value with `value.item()`:

```cpp
return masked_fill__cuda(self, mask, value.item());
```

`value.item()` copies one element from the GPU back to the host and blocks the calling thread until the copy lands. So every call with a device value stalls the host. The copy is also illegal during CUDA graph capture, so the op cannot be captured, and it forces a break in `torch.compile` cudagraph regions. You hit this when the fill value came from an earlier GPU computation, for example `x.masked_fill_(mask, scale)` where `scale` is a reduction result still on the device.

This PR reads the value on the GPU instead. For a device value it builds a `TensorIterator` over `self`, the broadcast mask, and the 0-dim value, and a small kernel selects the value where the mask is set. The 0-dim value broadcasts over the output, so there is no host round-trip and the op can be captured.

A CPU value never needed a sync, since `value.item()` there just reads host memory. So a CPU value keeps the old, cheaper path. Only a device value takes the new path, which is the case that used to stall.

The value is cast with `value.to(self.options())` before the kernel runs, so the result matches the existing scalar overload across the supported dtypes. The device, dtype, and overlap checks are preserved.

This follows recent host-sync removals in the CUDA backend, for example #186595 and #189305.

## Test Plan

```
python test/test_torch.py -k test_masked_fill_tensor_value
python test/test_torch.py -k test_masked_fill_tensor_value_cuda_graph
```

I also checked the change on an NVIDIA RTX A4000 (CUDA 12.1). I compiled the exact device-value path as an extension and compared it against the stock op:

- the device-value result matches the scalar overload for float32, float64, float16, bfloat16, and int64
- the stock `masked_fill_(mask, device_value)` fails inside `torch.cuda.graph` capture, while the on-device path captures and replays with the correct result

`lintrunner` is clean on the changed files.

This change was written with help from an AI assistant. I have read the diff and can explain every line.

input.title: [inductor] Select LPMF peak-memory nodes with a segment tree (O(n log n))
input.description:
## Summary

`reorder_for_peak_memory` runs on every `torch.compile` graph and defaults to on. It reorders nodes to keep peak memory low. The default strategy, `topological_sort_lpmf`, schedules nodes one at a time. To choose each node it scans the whole set of ready nodes with a Python `min()`.

That scan costs O(ready set) per node, so the pass is O(n * width). On a wide graph the ready set stays large for most of the run, and the pass turns quadratic. Compiled optimizers over a few thousand parameters, and graphs with many parallel branches, are the shapes that hit this.

A plain heap does not fit. The sort key's first term is `size if size > memory_gap else 0`, and `memory_gap` changes on every iteration, so the ordering shifts as you go.

This PR keeps a segment tree over the ready set. Adding a ready node, dropping a scheduled node, updating a node, and selecting are each O(log n), so the pass drops to O(n log n). Selection is two range-min queries: a prefix query over nodes with `size <= memory_gap`, then a full-range query if the prefix is empty. The node returned is the same one the old scan returned, down to the `index` tie-break. The schedule and its peak-memory result do not change. Only the default size-based strategy is touched. The opt-in successor-based branch keeps the old scan.

## Correctness

I checked equivalence two ways. A randomized data-structure test interleaves add, remove, and update against `select` over random `memory_gap` values, and asserts `select` returns the same node the linear `min()` would, across 300 trials. A state-machine harness then replays the whole pass on 400 random graphs and compares the pick at every step. All 400 produce a byte-identical schedule. Compiling a few models on CPU gives the same schedule and the same numbers as before.

## Benchmark

Selection-loop time on a wide graph, old scan against the segment tree:

| nodes | old scan | segment tree | speedup |
|------:|---------:|-------------:|--------:|
| 500   | 14.6 ms  | 4.3 ms       | 3.4x    |
| 1000  | 57.5 ms  | 9.3 ms       | 6.2x    |
| 2000  | 228.8 ms | 21.2 ms      | 10.8x   |
| 4000  | 918.0 ms | 44.5 ms      | 20.6x   |
| 8000  | 3993.5 ms| 103.9 ms     | 38.5x   |

Narrow graphs see no regression, since each `select` is a couple of O(log n) walks.

## Test Plan

```
python test/inductor/test_memory.py -k TestLpmfReadyQueue
```

`lintrunner` is clean on the changed files.

This change was written with help from an AI assistant. I have read the diff and can explain every line.


cc @voznesenskym @penguinwu @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @chauhang @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

input.title: [dynamo] Fix flaky test_compiled_autograd_id by resetting raw log after dynamo reset
input.description:
Fixes #190498.

## Root cause

`test_compiled_autograd_id` calls `torch._dynamo.reset()` mid-test to trigger an Inductor FX graph cache hit on the second compilation. `reset()` restarts `FRAME_COUNTER` and `COMPILE_COUNTER` from zero, so both compilations emit events with the same `frame_id=0` and `compiled_autograd_id=0` into the shared raw trace log. When `tlparse --strict` validates that log it sees duplicate compile IDs and fails non-deterministically (302 failures / 151 successes across 151 CI runs).

## Fix

Reset the raw log handler immediately after `torch._dynamo.reset()`, so `assertParses()` only inspects the second compilation's (cache-hit) events, which form a clean, duplicate-free trace. The `self.buffer` check on chromium events is unaffected because that handler spans both compilations.

An alternative would be to clear logging state inside `torch._dynamo.reset()`, but that is explicitly out of scope per the comment there: `NB: this does NOT reset logging state`.

## Test Plan

```
python test/dynamo/test_structured_trace.py StructuredTraceTest.test_compiled_autograd_id
```


cc @voznesenskym @penguinwu @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @kadeng @chauhang @amjames @jataylo @azahed98

input.title: [linalg] Missing 32-bit LAPACK workspace size checks in SVD, Eig, QR, and Lstsq
input.description:
### Description

While fixing `linalg.eigh` in PR #190006, we identified that several other linear algebra operations implemented via LAPACK on CPU do not have early validation checks to prevent 32-bit workspace integer overflows.

Because all default CPU builds link against a 32-bit integer LAPACK interface, passing a workspace query response `lwork` (which is returned as a float/double) that exceeds `INT32_MAX` (2,147,483,647) will silently overflow when cast to `int` inside `lapack_work_to_int`. This leads to undefined behavior, memory allocation errors, or backend failures with cryptic errors like:
```
RuntimeError: false INTERNAL ASSERT FAILED ...
linalg.svd: Argument 12 has illegal value.
Most certainly there is a bug in the implementation calling the backend library.
```

### Affected Operators & Formulas

1. **`linalg.svd` (`gesdd`)**:
   - Workspaces: `lwork` and `lrwork` (complex).
   - Under real divide-and-conquer `gesdd`, `lwork` requires roughly `3*min(m, n)^2 + max(max(m, n), 4*min(m, n)^2 + 4*min(m, n))` (when computing full `u` and `vt`).
   - For `m = n = 27,000`, the workspace exceeds `INT32_MAX`.

2. **`linalg.eig` (`geev`)**:
   - Non-symmetric eigenvalue decomposition also queries `lwork` and casts it through `lapack_work_to_int`.

3. **`linalg.qr` (`geqrf`)**:
   - Queries optimal workspace `lwork` and converts to `int` via `lapack_work_to_int`.

4. **`linalg.lstsq` (`gelsd`)**:
   - Divide-and-conquer least squares queries `lwork` and has no early overflow checks.

### Proposal
Implement early CPU size and workspace overflow checks in `TORCH_META_FUNC` for each of these functions, matching the check format added for `linalg.eigh` in PR #190006.

cc: @harsharajkumar-273


cc @jgong5 @mingfeima @XiaobingSuper @sanchitintel @ashokei @jingxu10 @aditew01 @malfet @jianyuh @nikitaved @mruberry @walterddr @Lezcano

input.title: Save result instead of input for inplace hardtanh backward
input.description:
Fixes #95432

## Summary

`hardtanh_` (inplace) saves a copy of the input tensor for backward, negating the memory savings that `inplace=True` should provide. This PR adds a separate `hardtanh_` derivative entry that references `result` instead of `self`, following the `leaky_relu_` pattern. Also fixes MPS boundary comparisons to match CPU/CUDA.

## Changes

1. `tools/autograd/derivatives.yaml`: Add `hardtanh_` inplace entry using `result`
2. `aten/src/ATen/native/mps/operations/Activation.mm`: Fix `>=`/`<=` comparisons
3. `test/test_nn.py`: Add inplace memory and correctness tests

## Context

- Prior PR #101387 was approved by @albanD but closed stale due to an MPS test failure. The MPS bug was that `hardtanh_backward_mps` used strict `>` / `<` comparisons instead of `>=` / `<=`, causing incorrect gradients at boundary values when given the result tensor. This PR fixes the MPS kernel as well.
- Root cause: PR #62329 changed `result` to `self` in the hardtanh derivative to fix XLA, which inadvertently broke inplace memory savings.
- This also fixes `ReLU6(inplace=True)` since `relu6_` dispatches to `hardtanh_`.

cc @albanD @soulitzer

input.title: Relax inductor forward-AD tolerance for linalg.householder_product f32
input.description:
Stack from [ghstack](https://github.com/ezyang/ghstack/tree/0.15.0) (oldest at bottom):
* __->__ #190505

TestCompositeComplianceCUDA.test_forward_ad_linalg_householder_product_cuda_float32
started failing on the inductor CI job (ciflow/inductor,
"unit-test / inductor-test / test (inductor, 2, 2)").

Root cause: #186388 ("Avoid native eager router graph breaks under
Dynamo") removed a Dynamo graph break, so more of this op's
composite-compliance forward-AD now runs through inductor instead of
partly in eager. Inductor's float32 codegen for the householder_product
JVP differs from the reference by ~1.3e-5 (a local inductor-vs-eager
probe of the tangent measured up to 5.7e-5), just past the default
float32 tolerance (atol=1e-5, rtol=1.3e-6). The failure tracks #186388
exactly (green before it landed and throughout its ~30h revert window,
red once relanded) and the reported diff is bit-identical on every
failing run, so it is deterministic on the CI a10g runner rather than
CUDA reduction noise; it does not reproduce on other GPUs, which is why
it is runner-specific.

The fix adds a scoped toleranceOverride (atol=2e-4, rtol=1e-4) for
float32, limited to TestCompositeCompliance/test_forward_ad on cuda and
gated on TEST_WITH_TORCHINDUCTOR so eager stays strict. The values give
comfortable margin over the observed drift and sit within the range of
the sibling linalg float32 overrides in this file.

Alternatives considered: reverting/adjusting #186388 (rejected -- it is
a wanted change and the exposed drift is benign inductor float32
numerics, not a correctness bug) and an unconditional override (rejected
to avoid loosening the eager test and masking future eager regressions).

Test Plan:

Eager (unaffected, passes):

```
python test/test_ops.py \
  TestCompositeComplianceCUDA.test_forward_ad_linalg_householder_product_cuda_float32
```

Inductor (the failing job; passes with the fix):

```
PYTORCH_TEST_WITH_INDUCTOR=1 python test/test_ops.py \
  TestCompositeComplianceCUDA.test_forward_ad_linalg_householder_product_cuda_float32
```

The CI-observed failure only reproduces on the inductor a10g runner, so
final validation is the ciflow/inductor job going green.

Authored with Claude.

input.title: [release/2.13] Keep static kernel owner alive in fast launcher
input.description:
Summary
Backport the static-launcher lifetime fix from upstream PR #188607 (0e67836a0c42c228e44e070267e2eb7472523fdf) to release/2.13.

_FastCudaLauncher stores a raw CUfunction, but _build_fast_launcher() replaces the bound runner and otherwise drops the only reference to the static kernel that owns the loaded CUDA module. The kernel can then be collected, its destructor unloads the module, and the cached launcher calls a dangling function pointer.

Keep the static kernel as _static_kernel_owner on the fast launcher and add a focused owner-lifetime regression test.

Impact
This fixes TorchInductor hard crashes observed after upgrading from Torch 2.11 to 2.13, including xdist workers terminating with CUDA illegal-instruction or segmentation-fault signals on an R575 B200 compatibility lane. No public API changes.

cc @voznesenskym @penguinwu @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @chauhang @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

instruction: Analyze this software issue and explain the possible cause and solution.

input.title: [SymmetricMemory] NVSHMEM backend: allocate signal pad within the same allocation as the buffer?
input.description:
### 🚀 The feature, motivation and pitch

### Proposal

Allocate the signal pad as part of the same allocation as the data buffer in
`NVSHMEMSymmetricMemory`, instead of a separate `nvshmem_malloc`. This mirrors
what `CUDASymmetricMemory` appears to do, and matches an existing
`// TODO: use the same allocation for signal pad` in the NVSHMEM backend
(torch/csrc/distributed/c10d/symm_mem/NVSHMEMSymmetricMemory.cu).

### Motivation

Every `nvshmem_malloc` is a collective, symmetric, barrier-synchronized
operation. Allocating the signal pad separately from the buffer therefore means:

- **2 collective allocations per symmetric tensor** instead of 1 — an extra
  cross-rank barrier on the allocation path.
- **2 order-sensitive allocation sites** that must stay consistent across all
  PEs, instead of a single site.

Folding the pad into the buffer allocation removes both, and aligns the NVSHMEM
backend's layout with the CUDA backend — which seems to be the intent of the
existing TODO.

I'm working on a SymmetricMemory-style backend for a non-CUDA accelerator whose
allocator has the same "every malloc is a collective barrier" semantics as
NVSHMEM, and I'd like to follow the direction PyTorch intends here before
committing to a layout.


### Alternatives

**Keep the separate / lazy signal pad allocation (current behavior).**
This avoids making every symmetric tensor carry a signal pad even when it never
participates in a signaling op. In practice the pad is small and configurable,
and symmetric allocations tend to be few and reused, so co-allocating eagerly
looks like a net win — but I may be missing a reason the current code keeps them
separate (e.g. the pad being sized lazily, VMM-backed heap considerations, or
the shared allocation-tracking structures used across backends).

A middle ground would be to make co-allocation and the pad size configurable, so
the eager layout can be turned off for workloads that allocate many tiny
symmetric tensors.


### Additional context

### Code references (please correct my understanding)

- `NVSHMEMSymmetricMemory` — separate `nvshmem_malloc` for the signal pad, plus
  the `// TODO: use the same allocation for signal pad`: <FILE:LINE permalink>
- `CUDASymmetricMemory` — signal pad within the same allocation block as the
  buffer: <FILE:LINE permalink, if found>

### Questions

1. Is the separate allocation intentional, or purely a not-yet-done TODO?
2. Are there known blockers? e.g. the cross-backend `AllocationRef` bookkeeping,
   the rendezvous handle exchange carrying `buffer_size` / `signal_pad_size`
   separately, `data_ptr`/offset handling for the returned tensor, or avoiding
   regressions in the CUDA backend.
3. Would a PR consolidating the NVSHMEM signal pad into the buffer allocation be
   welcome?

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
```

input.title: Fix cusparselt.cpp build when neither USE_CUSPARSELT nor USE_HIPSPARSELT is defined
input.description:
#178737 removed the file-level `#ifdef USE_CUSPARSELT` guard around `torch/csrc/cuda/shared/cusparselt.cpp` and only gates the `getVersionInt()` definition. The `cusparselt.def("getVersionInt", getVersionInt)` registration is now compiled unconditionally, so building the translation unit with neither macro defined fails:

```
cusparselt.cpp:56:35: error: use of undeclared identifier 'getVersionInt'
```

This restores a file-level `#if defined(USE_CUSPARSELT) || defined(USE_HIPSPARSELT)` guard around the file body, matching the source-gating condition in `torch/CMakeLists.txt`. The file compiles to an empty TU when neither macro is set, preserving the intended behavior.

instruction: Analyze this software issue and explain the possible cause and solution.

input.title: [MPS] Add logspace.out for the MPS backend
input.description:
Implements `aten::logspace.out` for the MPS backend. Fixes the `NotImplementedError` reported in #141287 for `torch.logspace(..., device='mps')`.

## Approach
`logspace` is composed on the existing `linspace_out_mps` kernel by filling `result` with the linear ramp `[start, end]`, then applying `base ** ramp` via `at::pow`. `logspace` is not perf-critical and `linspace`/`pow` already exists on MPS. The `steps == 0` and `steps == 1` edge cases mirror the CPU/CUDA implementations.

## Testing
Full `test_mps.py -k logspace` run is green (`OK`, expected failures=10 for the int cases). Coverage is handled via the standard MPS harness mechanisms:
- **Integer dtypes**: xfailed in `UNIMPLEMENTED_XFAILLIST`. The compose-on-linspace approach casts a float ramp, which rounds differently from CPU's integer computation (same underlying issue as linspace, #137635).
- **complex64**:  enabled via `SUPPORTED_COMPLEX_OPS`.
- **float16**: covered by the fp16 low-precision tolerance.
- **float32 / bfloat16**: pass at default tolerance.

Verified CPU-vs-MPS parity across `base` values and the `steps` edge cases.

Part of #141287.

input.title: [Stable C shim] Fix memory leak in StableIValue -> std::string conversion
input.description:
# Issue
- #174507, minor fix in the same area.
- Introduced in #168370, (66b4cf581fbfff3fb0ef107a36aa0de40d6805fe)  fyi @janeyx99 , @albanD 

# Summary
There's a memory leak in the function that converts StableIValue to std::string. On line 819 there's a heap allocation using a raw pointer stored in `strptr`, the return type is `std::string`, which copies the content by value. The pointer to the heap allocation can never leave the scope and as such is leaked, the returned value itself is correct.

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/7a247c9f4626c5bac80d45ed3a6e4c6eeba54941/torch/csrc/stable/stableivalue_conversions.h#L810-L824
 


I've verified the leak by introducing the first commit (09a0deae7266ea05c2e503f5a4e208290046a1b4) that adds a simple roundtrip to the `test_stable_ivalue.cpp` test, using the valgrind runner we created this does indeed show the memory leak:

```
$./test/cpp/shim/run_test.sh ./build/bin/
<snip>
==86539== 32 bytes in 1 blocks are definitely lost in loss record 883 of 3,144
==86539==    at 0x4849013: operator new(unsigned long) (in /usr/libexec/valgrind/vgpreload_memcheck-amd64-linux.so)
==86539==    by 0x12E378: TorchStableIValue_TestStringRoundtrip_Test::TestBody() (in /workspace/pytorch/build/bin/test_shim)
==86539==    by 0x1768F0: void testing::internal::HandleExceptionsInMethodIfSupported<testing::Test, void>(testing::Test*, void (testing::Test::*)(), char const*) (in /workspace/pytorch/build/bin/test_shim)
==86539==    by 0x160334: testing::Test::Run() [clone .part.0] (in /workspace/pytorch/build/bin/test_shim)
==86539==    by 0x160B14: testing::TestInfo::Run() (in /workspace/pytorch/build/bin/test_shim)
==86539==    by 0x161752: testing::TestSuite::Run() [clone .part.0] (in /workspace/pytorch/build/bin/test_shim)
==86539==    by 0x16A725: testing::internal::UnitTestImpl::RunAllTests() (in /workspace/pytorch/build/bin/test_shim)
==86539==    by 0x160FC4: testing::UnitTest::Run() (in /workspace/pytorch/build/bin/test_shim)
==86539==    by 0x12B062: main (in /workspace/pytorch/build/bin/test_shim)
==86539== 
==86539== LEAK SUMMARY:
==86539==    definitely lost: 32 bytes in 1 blocks
==86539==    indirectly lost: 0 bytes in 0 blocks
==86539==      possibly lost: 0 bytes in 0 blocks
==86539==    still reachable: 187,149 bytes in 3,215 blocks
==86539==         suppressed: 0 bytes in 0 blocks
==86539== Reachable blocks (those to which a pointer was found) are not shown.
==86539== To see them, rerun with: --leak-check=full --show-leak-kinds=all
```

The second commit (3c4256a151798918918dcba2354d542900166289) removes the `new` allocation and just puts the string on the stack. Valgrind shows there's no leaks for that commit:

```
==92625== LEAK SUMMARY:
==92625==    definitely lost: 0 bytes in 0 blocks
==92625==    indirectly lost: 0 bytes in 0 blocks
==92625==      possibly lost: 0 bytes in 0 blocks
==92625==    still reachable: 187,149 bytes in 3,215 blocks
==92625==         suppressed: 0 bytes in 0 blocks
```

I discovered this just now while adding the [`div.Tensor_mode`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/v2.13.0/aten/src/ATen/native/native_functions.yaml#L2106-L2112) operation to my [flash_powder](https://github.com/iwanders/flash_powder) bindings. That doesn't actually use this conversion directly, but it is also exercised [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/7a247c9f4626c5bac80d45ed3a6e4c6eeba54941/torch/csrc/shim_common.cpp#L295-L298) by the `to_ivalue` function that's called by the dispatcher for string arguments. So this leak showed up when I was testing my newly added modulo operation. So a pretty lucky find, and an easy fix :)

input.title: [MPS] Fix native_layer_norm for small-variance rows via two-pass kernel
input.description:
Fixes: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/190491

Addresses the cancellation issue from one-pass kernel computing variance as E[x²] − E[x]² with the numerically stable two-pass version E[(x−μ)²]. The case where per-thread row slices can be cached in thread memory avoids doing the read twice has its own single row kernel and the looped kernel exists for the general case.

Benchmarked on an M2 Max, macOS27 beta.
```
  ┌───────────────┬────────────┬───────────────┬─────────────────┬───────┐
  │ shape (M × N) │   kernel   │ baseline (µs) │ fix, ulong (µs) │   Δ   │
  ├───────────────┼────────────┼───────────────┼─────────────────┼───────┤
  │ 50432 × 1024  │ single_row │ 1362          │ 1169            │ −14%  │
  ├───────────────┼────────────┼───────────────┼─────────────────┼───────┤
  │ 16384 × 4096  │ single_row │ 2126          │ 1896            │ −11%  │
  ├───────────────┼────────────┼───────────────┼─────────────────┼───────┤
  │ 8192 × 8192   │ looped     │ 2163          │ 2272            │ +5.0% │
  ├───────────────┼────────────┼───────────────┼─────────────────┼───────┤
  │ 4096 × 16384  │ looped     │ 2189          │ 2370            │ +8.3% │
  ├───────────────┼────────────┼───────────────┼─────────────────┼───────┤
  │ 4096 × 32768  │ looped     │ 4390          │ 4595            │ +4.7% │
  └───────────────┴────────────┴───────────────┴─────────────────┴───────┘

```

Adds support for 64-bit indexing while we are here. Avoids the silent overflow.

Two test cases, one addressing the cancellation issue and one for the new indexing.

instruction: Analyze this software issue and explain the possible cause and solution.

input.title: [MPS] layer_norm forward kernel variance computation causes divergence from CPU reference
input.description:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

The MPS forward kernel computes variance in one pass as `E[x²] − E[x]²` and then clamps `var < 1e-6` to zero. For a row with nearly identical values, the two quantities become nearly equal (and possibly large), so the small true variance is lost to rounding noise. Clamping it to zero below a certain level is just a band-aid to avoid the unrealistic case of negative variance, with the negative side effect of also setting valid positive but small variances to zero. This collapses reciprocal of standard deviation to `rsqrt(eps)` and makes the normalized outputs diverge between MPS backend and CPU.

Repro for the issue from 
```python
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

# Per-element variance ~9.8e-7 just below the kernel's 1e-6 clamp
# with the eps that ViT-family LayerNorms use (1e-12).
x = torch.tensor([[-9.9e-4, -9.9e-4, 9.9e-4, 9.9e-4]])
print(F.layer_norm(x,            (4,), eps=1e-12))   # CPU
print(F.layer_norm(x.to('mps'),  (4,), eps=1e-12))   # MPS

# Output:
# tensor([[-1.0000, -1.0000,  1.0000,  1.0000]])
# tensor([[-990., -990.,  990.,  990.]], device='mps:0')
```

The fix to avoid the cancellation is to compute the subtraction before the square by using the other identity for variance `E[(x−μ)²]`. This requires rewriting the kernel in a two-pass formulation, but also removes the need for the clamping as we can no longer get negative variances due to the cancellation.

Opening a PR with the suggested changes.

### Versions

Current nightly. MacOS27 beta.

cc @kulinseth @malfet @DenisVieriu97 @aditvenk @Isalia20

input.title: Improve mean time to detect the issue by adding warning logs when users miss to call destroy_process_group
input.description:
While I was working on some project, I missed to call destroy_process_group and got error message "terminate called without an active exception". I have written a minimal repro script.

```python
import torch
import os
import torch.multiprocessing as mp
import sys
import time

WORLD_SIZE=5
MASTER_PORT=8118
MASTER_ADDR="127.0.0.1"

def dist_body(rank, result_queue):
    try:
        os.environ["MASTER_ADDR"] = MASTER_ADDR
        os.environ["MASTER_PORT"] = str(MASTER_PORT)
        torch.distributed.init_process_group(
            backend="gloo",
            rank=rank,
            world_size=WORLD_SIZE
        )
        pg = torch.distributed.group.WORLD
        rank_pg = torch.distributed.get_rank(pg)

        # Sleep so that we can use gdb to see the backtrace and check where std::terminate was called
        # time.sleep(20) 

        input_tensors = torch.tensor([rank_pg * 100], dtype=torch.float32)
        output_tensors = [torch.empty(1, dtype=torch.float32) for i in range(WORLD_SIZE)]
        pg.allgather(output_tensors, input_tensors)
        status = f"ok, rank_pg={rank_pg}"
        result_queue.put((rank, status))
    except Exception as e:
        print(e) 
        result_queue.put((rank, f"failed : {e}"))
    finally:
        # Remove destroy process group to reproduce the issue
        torch.distributed.destroy_process_group()


def dist_main():
    world_size = WORLD_SIZE

    ctx = mp.get_context("spawn")
    result_queue = ctx.Queue()
    mp.spawn(
        dist_body,
        args=(result_queue,),
        nprocs= world_size, 
        join=True
    )
    results = [result_queue.get() for _ in range(world_size)]
    for rank, status in sorted(results):
        print(rank, status)
        assert status == f"ok, rank_pg={rank}"

if __name__ == "__main__":
    dist_main()
```

I only realized my simple mistake after debugging for very long time after checking the backtrace using gdb.
```txt
(gdb) bt
#-1  __pthread_kill_implementation (no_tid=0, signo=6, threadid=<optimized out>) at ./nptl/pthread_kill.c:44
#0  __pthread_kill_internal (signo=6, threadid=<optimized out>) at ./nptl/pthread_kill.c:78
#1  __GI___pthread_kill (threadid=<optimized out>, signo=signo@entry=6) at ./nptl/pthread_kill.c:89
#2  0x0000790a4304527e in __GI_raise (sig=sig@entry=6) at ../sysdeps/posix/raise.c:26
#3  0x0000790a430288ff in __GI_abort () at ./stdlib/abort.c:79
#4  0x0000790a116a5ff5 in ?? () from /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libstdc++.so.6
#5  0x0000790a116bb0da in ?? () from /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libstdc++.so.6
#6  0x0000790a116a5a55 in std::terminate() () from /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libstdc++.so.6
#7  0x0000790a116a592a in ?? () from /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libstdc++.so.6
#8  0x0000790a41c1fce5 in ?? () from /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libgcc_s.so.1
#9 0x0000790a41c203c0 in _Unwind_ForcedUnwind () from /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libgcc_s.so.1
#10 0x0000790a430a57a4 in __GI___pthread_unwind (buf=<optimized out>) at ./nptl/unwind.c:130
#11 0x0000790a4309dd22 in __do_cancel () at ../sysdeps/nptl/pthreadP.h:271
#12 __GI___pthread_exit (value=0x0) at ./nptl/pthread_exit.c:36
#13 0x00000000004bb73a in PyThread_exit_thread ()
#14 0x00000000004a51d3 in ?? ()
#15 0x00000000006a6e8b in PyEval_AcquireThread ()
#16 0x00007909b8ffb3ed in pybind11::gil_scoped_acquire::gil_scoped_acquire() ()
   from /home/irshadcc/Downloads/torch-dev-env/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/lib/libtorch_python.so
#17 0x00007909b936ed6b in torch::detail::(anonymous namespace)::ConcretePyInterpreterVTable::decref(_object*) const ()
   from /home/irshadcc/Downloads/torch-dev-env/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/lib/libtorch_python.so
#18 0x0000790a41740545 in c10::TensorImpl::decref_pyobject() const ()
--Type <RET> for more, q to quit, c to continue without paging--
   from /home/irshadcc/Downloads/torch-dev-env/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/lib/libc10.so
#19 0x00007909aa0ab9e5 in c10d::(anonymous namespace)::AsyncAllgatherWork::~AsyncAllgatherWork() ()
   from /home/irshadcc/Downloads/torch-dev-env/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/lib/libtorch_cpu.so
#20 0x00007909aa0be97f in c10d::ProcessGroupGloo::runLoop(int) ()
   from /home/irshadcc/Downloads/torch-dev-env/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/lib/libtorch_cpu.so
#21 0x0000790a116ecdb4 in ?? () from /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libstdc++.so.6
#22 0x0000790a4309caa4 in start_thread (arg=<optimized out>) at ./nptl/pthread_create.c:447
#23 0x0000790a43129c6c in clone3 () at ../sysdeps/unix/sysv/linux/x86_64/clone3.S:78
```


 
It turns out that during the destruction of AsyncAllgatherWork, it tried to decref the tensors after the python interpreter was finalized. 

The proper fix should be to destroy the process group, but I think we can at least improve the mean time to detect this issue by adding some warning logs in pytorch. I have already tried using CPP_BACKTRACE and TORCH_SHOW_CPP_STACKTRACES and it didn't work. 

One of the solutions that I have came up with is that we check if the python interpreter is already destroyed or not before decref and add torch warning logs. 

```C++
void ConcretePyInterpreterVTable::decref(PyObject* pyobj, ...) const {
  // If the interpreter is finalizing, a non-main thread cannot safely take
  // the GIL — doing so gets it forcibly killed via pthread_exit, which unwinds
  // through this noexcept frame and calls std::terminate. Leak the ref instead;
  // the process is going away and the memory is about to be reclaimed anyway.
  if (_Py_IsFinalizing()) {
   TORCH_WARN("Python interpreter is already destroyed ........");
    return;
  }
  pybind11::gil_scoped_acquire gil;
  ...
}
```

I would love to open a PR to fix this issue after discussing the all feasible solutions.






cc @awgu @wanchaol @fegin @fduwjj @wz337 @wconstab @d4l3k @pragupta @msaroufim @dcci @aditvenk @weifengpy

instruction: Analyze this software issue and explain the possible cause and solution.

input.title: Zero fully-masked rows in fastpath masked_softmax to match SDPA
input.description:
Fixes a silent divergence between the native transformer fast path and the SDPA fallback for rows whose every key is masked.

### Problem

`nn.TransformerEncoder` / `nn.MultiheadAttention` in eval: when a bool `attn_mask` combines with `src_key_padding_mask` such that some rows end up with all keys masked (e.g. tail padding deeper than a banded causal mask), the two execution paths disagree:

- **Native fast path** (`_transformer_encoder_layer_fwd` → `native_multi_head_attention_*` → `masked_softmax`): those rows come out **NaN** (softmax over the empty set).
- **SDPA fallback** (`F.multi_head_attention_forward` → `scaled_dot_product_attention` → `_safe_softmax`): the same rows are **zero-filled**.

Same module, same inputs, no warning — outputs differ between CPU/CUDA (fast path) and MPS (always falls back), and flip with `torch.backends.mha.set_fastpath_enabled`. The existing `test_multiheadattention_fastpath_attn_mask` acknowledges the mismatch and works around it with `.nan_to_num()`.

Minimal repro:

```python
import torch, torch.nn as nn

torch.manual_seed(0)
S, WINDOW, PAD = 16, 4, 12          # pad depth > band width => fully-masked rows
enc = nn.TransformerEncoder(
    nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(32, 4, 64, batch_first=True, norm_first=True),
    1, enable_nested_tensor=False).eval()
x = torch.randn(1, S, 32)
i, j = torch.arange(S)[:, None], torch.arange(S)[None, :]
banded = ~((i - j >= 0) & (i - j < WINDOW))
kpm = torch.zeros(1, S, dtype=torch.bool)
kpm[:, -PAD:] = True
out = enc(x, mask=banded, src_key_padding_mask=kpm)
print(out[:, -PAD:].isnan().any())   # True on the fast path, False with fastpath disabled / on MPS
```

### Fix

Adopt the `_safe_softmax` convention at the single site shared by the CPU and CUDA fast paths — the `masked_softmax` wrapper in `aten/src/ATen/native/transformers/attention.cpp`: after `_masked_softmax`, zero the rows whose mask is all-True along the key dim. The kpm `[B, S]` layout (`mask_type == 1`) is viewed to `[B, 1, 1, S]` exactly as the kernels broadcast it; `[L, S]` and 4-D masks broadcast as-is through `all(-1, keepdim=True)`.

Zeroing the softmax row reproduces the SDPA path's semantics: zero attention probabilities → zero context row from the following bmm → identical out-proj/residual downstream. Verified empirically: with the patch, fast path == fastpath-disabled to machine precision on the repro, and CPU == MPS.

An alternative — fixing inside each `_masked_softmax` kernel (CPU `SoftMax.cpp` + CUDA) — avoids the extra mask reduction but triples the surface; the wrapper reduction is O(mask) next to the attention matmuls.

### Tests

- New `test_transformerencoder_fastpath_fully_masked_rows_match_sdpa`: banded bool mask + deep key padding, asserts the fast path output is finite and equal to the fastpath-disabled reference.
- `test_multiheadattention_fastpath_attn_mask`: the `.nan_to_num()` workaround is now dead and removed — the test asserts direct equality.
- `pytest test/test_transformers.py`: 3322 passed, 105 skipped, 1 failure (`test_transformer_encoder_layer_fwd_fake_cpu`) that is environmental on my machine — inductor's runtime C++ compile fails on `omp.h` missing from Apple clang, unrelated to this change.

instruction: Analyze this software issue and explain the possible cause and solution.

input.title: Fix CUDA IPC dataset cleanup in DataLoader workers
input.description:
## Author Notes

Fix for https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/190268 , I think that's the simplest one even though we have to change the calling convention

## Agent Notes

DataLoader worker processes retain their launch arguments until process exit, while `get_worker_info()` also holds the dataset globally. For CUDA datasets received over IPC, multiprocessing exits without running the storage deleters, leaving producer-side CUDA IPC reference counts elevated and allocations in limbo. This change passes the dataset through a mutable holder consumed at worker startup and clears the global worker info on worker-loop exit, releasing CUDA IPC storage while cleanup remains operational without relying on private multiprocessing fields.

Test plan:

```bash
PYTORCH_TEST_CUDA_MEM_LEAK_CHECK=1 PYTORCH_TEST_WITH_SLOW_GRADCHECK=1 python test/test_dataloader.py TestDataLoaderDeviceTypeCUDA.test_sparse_tensor_multiprocessing_context_forkserver_cuda
```

The reproducer passes. Focused DataLoader lint checks pass; full `spin lint` reports unrelated pre-existing Pyrefly failures in `torch/_dynamo/symbolic_convert.py` and `torch/_dynamo/variables/constant.py`.

This PR was authored with an AI assistant.

input.title: [MPS]: add handling for empty batch dimensions in FFT operations
input.description:
### Description

fixes https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/190011

on MPS, FFT operations failed for tensors with an empty batch dimension because **MPSGraph** rejects empty tensor placeholders; MPS FFT implementations attempted to construct and execute a graph even though the output contained no elements.

fixed this by resizing the output to its expected shape and returning early when it is empty in the complex-to-complex, real-to-complex, and complex-to-real MPS FFT paths, empty transform dimensions remain rejected by the FFT frontend

### Test Plan

```bash
python test/test_mps.py -v -k empty_batch_fft
```

input.title: [MPS] Fix torch.nextafter for bfloat16
input.description:
On MPS, `torch.nextafter` was returning its input unchanged for `bfloat16`, because  `nextafter_functor` in `BinaryKernel.metal` called `::metal::nextafter`, which does not have a `bfloat` overload.
This caused the arguments to be promoted to `float`, stepped by one fp32 ULP, and then cast back to `bfloat`, which rounded the step away.

Fixed by implementing a bitwise manual stepping overload for `bfloat` inside `nextafter_functor` in `BinaryKernel.metal` using its `ushort` representation, closely mirroring the CPU implementation in `c10/util/BFloat16-math.h` and extend nextafter unit test to cover bfloat as well


Fixes https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/190476

input.title: [ATen] Reject non-positive kernel_size in fractional_max_pool
input.description:
## Related Issue

Related to #190272, complements #190475

## Why

The C++ structured metas for `fractional_max_pool2d/3d` never validate `kernel_size >= 1`, so direct aten calls in eager silently return `-inf` outputs with out-of-range indices. #190475 adds the check at the Python API and 2d meta layer; this covers the remaining raw-aten paths (2d/3d eager on all backends, and the 3d compile path which has no Python meta).

## How

- Add `TORCH_CHECK(poolSize... > 0)` to the structured metas in `FractionalMaxPool2d.cpp` and `FractionalMaxPool3d.cpp`, matching the `max_pool` "kernel size should be greater than zero" phrasing
- Add a raw-aten regression test in `test/nn/test_pooling.py`

cc @mikaylagawarecki

instruction: Analyze this software issue and explain the possible cause and solution.

input.title: Mark intrusive_ptr copy constructors noexcept
input.description: The copy constructors of `intrusive_ptr` and `weak_intrusive_ptr` only copy a raw pointer and call `retain_()`, but the signatures were never annotated, while the move constructors and all assignment operators already are. `intrusive_ptr::retain_` is already `noexcept`; `weak_intrusive_ptr::retain_` (an atomic weakcount increment plus a debug-only internal assert, same shape as the strong counterpart and as its `noexcept` sibling `reset_`) gets the annotation too. This lets `std::is_nothrow_copy_constructible`/`noexcept(...)` queries in generic code see the true guarantee.

instruction: Analyze this software issue and explain the possible cause and solution.

input.title: [MPS] `torch.nextafter` is a no-op for bfloat16
input.description:
## 🐛 Describe the bug

On MPS, `torch.nextafter` returns its input unchanged for bfloat16. A sweep over {±0, ±1, ±2, ±smallest normal, ±denormal, max, min, ±inf, nan} pairs gives 180/225 bitwise mismatches vs CPU; the same sweep for float32/float16 matches CPU exactly (denormal handling aside, tracked in #125051).

```python
import torch

a = torch.tensor(1.0, dtype=torch.bfloat16)
b = torch.tensor(2.0, dtype=torch.bfloat16)

torch.nextafter(a, b)                      # 1.0078125 (CPU, correct)
torch.nextafter(a.to("mps"), b.to("mps"))  # 1.0 (unchanged — wrong)
```

Likely cause: `nextafter_functor` in `aten/src/ATen/native/mps/kernels/BinaryKernel.metal` calls `::metal::nextafter`, which has no bfloat overload; the arguments promote to `float`, step one fp32 ULP, and the cast back to bfloat rounds the step away. CPU implements exact bf16 stepping in `c10/util/BFloat16-math.h`, and the MPS kernel does register a bfloat variant, so this doesn't look intentional.

Tests miss it because `test_mps.py::test_nextafter` only covers float16/float32 (its `nextafter(x, y) > x` assertion fails for bfloat16), and OpInfo comparisons can't see 1-ULP errors (see #125028). Related: #124985, #187340.

Reproduced on 2.13.0 and nightly 2.14.0.dev20260718 (Apple M1 Pro).



## Versions



```
PyTorch version: 2.14.0.dev20260718
Is debug build: False
CUDA used to build PyTorch: None
ROCM used to build PyTorch: N/A

OS: macOS 27.0 (arm64)
GCC version: Could not collect
Clang version: 21.0.0 (clang-2100.3.25.1)
CMake version: Could not collect
Libc version: N/A

Python version: 3.12.13 (main, Mar 25 2026, 03:16:06) [Clang 22.1.1 ] (64-bit runtime)
Python platform: macOS-27.0-arm64-arm-64bit
Is CUDA available: False
GPU models and configuration: No CUDA
Is XPU available: False
Is XNNPACK available: False

CPU: Apple M1 Pro
```

cc @kulinseth @malfet @DenisVieriu97 @jhavukainen @aditvenk @Isalia20

input.title: [Inductor] Validate kernel_size is positive in fractional_max_pool
input.description:
## Related Issue

Fixes #190272

## Why

`fractional_max_pool2d/3d` never validate `kernel_size >= 1`. With a non-positive kernel, eager silently returns `-inf` outputs and out-of-range indices, while the inductor lowering emits the index `in_sz - kernel_sz` (out of bounds for the input), so the generated kernel crashes at runtime with `index out of bounds`.

## How

- Raise `ValueError` on non-positive `kernel_size` in `fractional_max_pool2d_with_indices` and `fractional_max_pool3d_with_indices`, matching how other pooling ops reject invalid kernels
- Add the same check to `meta_fractional_max_pool2d` so direct aten calls under compile fail at trace time instead of generating an out-of-bounds kernel
- Add regression tests in `test/inductor/test_torchinductor.py` and `test/nn/test_pooling.py`
- Register `fractional_max_pool2d/3d_with_indices` as untraceable (`PROXY_ITERATED`) in `test/test_fx.py`, since the new validation iterates `kernel_size`, which FX symbolic tracing of the bare functional cannot trace (same as the existing `fractional_max_pool2d/3d` entries)

cc @mikaylagawarecki @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

input.title: [Inductor] Add complex dtype coverage for nan_to_num OpInfo
input.description:
Depends on #190428 (included as the first commit; will rebase once it lands).

## Why

`nan_to_num` supports complex in eager but is never tested for it: complex is missing from its OpInfo dtypes, eager autograd rejects complex at forward, and MPS auto-xfails it despite working.

## How

- Add complex64/128 to the `nan_to_num` OpInfo dtypes

input.title: Fix broken conditions and typos in CMake
input.description:
Small fixes to conditions and settings that were silently doing nothing:

- **`BLAS=Generic` env check** (cmake/Dependencies.cmake): `if(ENV{GENERIC_BLAS_LIBRARIES} STREQUAL "")` is missing the `$` and always took the else branch, so `BLAS=Generic` without the env var gave `find_library` no candidates and failed at generate time. Fixing it restores the pre-#74269 default of looking for `libblas`; the Spack use case that sets the env var is unaffected.
- **`CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED`** (CMakeLists.txt): was set without the `CMAKE_` prefix (and only on Linux), i.e. never took effect; now declared next to `CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD`. All supported compilers accept `-std=c++20`, so this only turns a hypothetical silent standard fallback into an error.
- **`TORCH_DEFAULT_VERSION` fallback** (CMakeLists.txt): `set(TORCH_DEFAULT_VERSION, "0.0.0")` set a variable literally named with a trailing comma; the branch is currently unreachable but now works as intended.
- **message modes** (CMakeLists.txt): `message(Warning ...)` and `message(ERROR ...)` are not valid modes and printed as NOTICE text. The aarch64 `-Og` note becomes a real WARNING; requesting `USE_CPP_CODE_COVERAGE` with a compiler lacking coverage support now fails at configure time instead of silently producing an uninstrumented build (GCC and all Clang variants unaffected).
- **BUNDLE_PTXAS guard** (CMakeLists.txt): checked a path the code never produces, so it was always true; `file(COPY)` is already incremental, the guard is dropped.
- **c10/xpu `BUILD_LIBTORCHLESS` guard**: inverted relative to c10/cuda and c10/hip; regular builds ran a pointless `find_library` while libtorchless builds never set `C10_XPU_LIB`. Aligned with the cuda/hip pattern.

instruction: Analyze this software issue and explain the possible cause and solution.

input.title: Remove unreachable CMake branches
input.description:
Three branches that can no longer execute:

- **pre-CUDA-12.1 `--source-in-ptx`** (CMakeLists.txt): cmake/public/cuda.cmake rejects CUDA < 12.1 with FATAL_ERROR, so the `VERSION_LESS 12.1` condition is always false. Not passing the flag on 12.1+ is the intended behavior from #102756 (nvcc segfaults on it, #102372), so it is not promoted to unconditional; `-lineinfo` is kept.
- **`-Wunused-private-field` skip** (cmake/public/utils.cmake): #150077 replaced the individual `-Wunused-*` flags with `-Wunused`, so the skipped flag never appears in `private_compile_options`. The still-live `-Wextra-semi` skip is kept.
- **duplicate CUDA+ROCm mutual-exclusion check** (aten/src/ATen/CMakeLists.txt): aten/CMakeLists.txt performs the same FATAL_ERROR (after LoadHIP, on the final USE_ROCM value) before `add_subdirectory(src/ATen)`, so the inner copy cannot fire.

instruction: Analyze this software issue and explain the possible cause and solution.

input.title: Remove always-false CMake blocks caused by typos
input.description:
Four blocks whose conditions have never been true because of a typo, each with a compensating mechanism that made the breakage invisible:

- **uv_a include workaround** (cmake/Dependencies.cmake, from #77312): the condition misspells `USE_DISTRIBUTED` as `USE_DISTRIBUED`. Gloo gets the vendored libuv headers via the directory-level `include_directories` above it.
- **USE_ROCM_CK_SDPA env override** (aten/src/ATen/CMakeLists.txt, from #178310): `if(ENV{USE_ROCM_CK_SDPA} EQUAL 1)` is missing a `$`. EnvVarForwarding.cmake already forwards the env var into the cache and the `cmake_dependent_option` default is ON; removing the block also stops the `elseif(NOT WIN32)` force-ON from clobbering an explicit `-DUSE_ROCM_CK_SDPA=OFF`.
- **ROCM_SOURCE_DIR fallback** (caffe2/CMakeLists.txt, from #120898): `if($ROCM_SOURCE_DIR STREQUAL "")` is missing braces. The include it fed was removed in #152569; the only remaining consumer was a malformed `message(INFO ...)`.
- **CMAKE_LINKER_FLAGS_DEBUG appends** (CMakeLists.txt): not a real CMake variable (the real ones are `CMAKE_{EXE,SHARED,MODULE}_LINKER_FLAGS_DEBUG`); the flags are codegen flags already applied via the adjacent `CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_DEBUG` appends.

Deleting rather than fixing the typos preserves shipped behavior instead of activating years-dormant, untested code paths (a fixed CK SDPA condition would force it ON on Windows, defeating #183962).

instruction: Analyze this software issue and explain the possible cause and solution.

input.title: Fix always-false env check in BLAS=Generic branch
input.description: `if(ENV{GENERIC_BLAS_LIBRARIES} STREQUAL "")` is missing the `$`, so it compares a literal string and always takes the else branch. With the env var unset, `GENERIC_BLAS` ends up empty and `find_library(BLAS_LIBRARIES NAMES)` has no candidates, so `BLAS=Generic` without `GENERIC_BLAS_LIBRARIES` fails at generate time with a NOTFOUND error. Fixing the check restores the pre-#74269 default of looking for `libblas`. Users who do set the env var (the Spack use case #74269 was added for) are unaffected.

instruction: Analyze this software issue and explain the possible cause and solution.

input.title: Fix inert typos in top-level CMakeLists.txt
input.description:
Three small fixes, none changing behavior for currently supported configurations:

- `set(CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED ON)` was missing the `CMAKE_` prefix (and was Linux-only), so it never did anything; now `CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED ON` sits next to `CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD`. All supported compilers accept `-std=c++20`, so this only turns a hypothetical silent standard fallback into an error.
- `set(TORCH_DEFAULT_VERSION, "0.0.0")` set a variable literally named `TORCH_DEFAULT_VERSION,`; the fallback branch is currently unreachable (version.txt is checked in, and `file(READ)` errors first if it were missing), but now it would actually work.
- `message(Warning ...)` and `message(ERROR ...)` are not valid message modes and printed as NOTICE text. The aarch64 `-Og` note becomes a real WARNING, and requesting `USE_CPP_CODE_COVERAGE` with a compiler that has no coverage support now fails at configure time instead of silently producing an uninstrumented build (GCC and all Clang variants are unaffected).

input.title: Remove appends to nonexistent CMAKE_LINKER_FLAGS_DEBUG
input.description: `CMAKE_LINKER_FLAGS_DEBUG` is not a CMake variable (the real ones are `CMAKE_{EXE,SHARED,MODULE}_LINKER_FLAGS_DEBUG`), so these appends have never reached the linker. Nothing is lost: `-fno-omit-frame-pointer` and `-Og`/`-O0` are code-generation flags that non-LTO link invocations ignore anyway, and the compile side is already covered by the adjacent `CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_DEBUG` appends. Deleting rather than renaming avoids changing link-time codegen in untested Debug+LTO configurations.

The `target_include_directories` this variable used to feed was removed in #152569, leaving a malformed `message(INFO ...)` (INFO is not a message mode) as the only consumer. input.title: Remove dead USE_ROCM_CK_SDPA env override block
input.description:
The `if(ENV{USE_ROCM_CK_SDPA} EQUAL 1)` condition is missing a `$`, so it compares a literal string and has never been true: whenever the env var is set (to 0 or 1), this block does nothing. It was never missed because the intended semantics are already covered elsewhere — cmake/EnvVarForwarding.cmake forwards `USE_ROCM_CK_SDPA` from the environment into the cache before options are declared, and the `cmake_dependent_option` default is already ON for ROCm non-Windows builds.

Deleting the block rather than fixing the `$` avoids two regressions: a fixed condition would force CK SDPA ON on Windows (the env branch has no WIN32 guard, defeating the exclusion from #183962), and the live `elseif(NOT WIN32)` force-ON silently clobbered an explicit `-DUSE_ROCM_CK_SDPA=OFF`. With the block gone, an explicit opt-out is honored; all other configurations behave as before. The gfx942/gfx950 arch check is kept.

cc @jeffdaily @sunway513 @jithunnair-amd @pruthvistony @ROCmSupport @jataylo @hongxiayang @naromero77amd @pragupta @jerrymannil @xinyazhang

instruction: Analyze this software issue and explain the possible cause and solution.

input.title: Fix _scaled_mm dropping scale_result on the fp8 output path
input.description:
Fixes #190449.

### Summary

`torch._scaled_mm(..., out_dtype=torch.float8_e4m3fn, scale_result=s)` silently ignores `scale_result`: the fp32 accumulator is cast to fp8 at an implicit scale of 1.0 regardless of `s`. This is a regression against 2.9.1, which honored it; it broke in the `Blas.cpp` -> `ScaledBlas.cpp` refactor and is present on 2.10, nightly, and main.

### Root cause

The v1 entry `_scaled_mm_out_cuda` validates `scale_result` but never forwards it. It calls `_scaled_gemm` with no `scale_result`, and `_scaled_gemm` hardwires the result-scale slot of `cublasCommonArgs` to `std::nullopt`, so `CUBLASLT_MATMUL_DESC_D_SCALE_POINTER` is never set. The in-tree doc comment still promises the behavior for fp8 output.

### Fix

Thread `scale_result` through: `_scaled_gemm` gains an optional `scale_result` parameter (default `std::nullopt`, so the v2 recipe call sites are unchanged) and passes it into `cublasCommonArgs`; the v1 path forwards its `scale_result`. Only the v1 tensorwise path opts in; v2 scaling recipes keep the previous behavior.

### Test

Added `test_float8_scale_result` in `test/test_scaled_matmul_cuda.py`, using integer operands so the accumulation and its 0.5x scaling are exact in e4m3 (any deviation is a real bug, not rounding):

```
python test/test_scaled_matmul_cuda.py -k test_float8_scale_result
```

The regression and the fixed behavior were confirmed on an H100: the repro in #190449 returns unscaled fp8 on 2.12 nightly (bug) and correctly scaled fp8 on 2.9.1 (correct). The patched source was not built locally (no CUDA on the dev machine), so build-and-test verification is left to CI.

This PR was authored with assistance from Claude.

input.title: Remove dead build options and unused CMake variables
input.description:
All removed items have no remaining consumers:

- `USE_NNAPI`: last consumer was `caffe2/mobile/`, removed in #125818
- `USE_PROF`: gated caffe2 htrace profiling, removed in #14117 and #125038; no Findhtrace module exists
- `USE_MKLDNN_CBLAS`: no longer passed to mkl-dnn since the DNNL v1.2 upgrade (#32422); modern oneDNN has no external-CBLAS option
- `USE_TENSORRT` and `BUILD_NVFUSER`: Summary.cmake lines missed by the cleanups in #126542 and #111447, now printing undefined variables
- `MSVC_RUNTIME_LIBRARY_OPTION`: expands to nothing; its setter (named `MSVC_RUNTIME_LIBRARY_FLAG` in #10359) was removed in #18548
- `PYTHON_LIB_REL_PATH`: last consumer removed in #129067
- `AT_CUDA_ENABLED`: its Config.h.in consumer was removed in #7275 in favor of runtime CUDAHooks
- `CUDA_LINK_LIBRARIES_KEYWORD`: only read by FindCUDA's `cuda_add_library`, unused since the native CUDA language migration (#62445); the guarded targets are plain `add_library` now

instruction: Analyze this software issue and explain the possible cause and solution.

input.title: _scaled_mm: scale_result silently ignored on fp8 output path (CUDA, torch 2.10 / nightly / main)
input.description:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

`torch._scaled_mm(..., out_dtype=torch.float8_e4m3fn, scale_result=s)` no longer applies `scale_result` to the fp8 output: the fp32 accumulator is cast to fp8 at an implicit scale of 1.0 regardless of `s`. The v1 entry point still validates `scale_result` ("must be a float scalar") but never forwards it. In `_scaled_mm_out_cuda` the call to `_scaled_gemm(...)` omits `scale_result`, and `_scaled_gemm` hardwires the result-scale slot of `cublasCommonArgs` to `std::nullopt`, so `CUBLASLT_MATMUL_DESC_D_SCALE_POINTER` is never set. The in-tree doc comment still promises the behavior ("`scale_result`: a scalar tensor with the scale of the output, only utilized if the output is a float8 type"), so a caller that requests fp8 output with an output scale silently gets unscaled fp8, which can saturate/flush and corrupt downstream.

This is a regression: torch 2.9.1+cu128 honors `scale_result` (the pre-refactor path forwarded `args.scale_result_ptr` into `scaled_gemm`); it broke in the `Blas.cpp` -> `ScaledBlas.cpp` refactor between 2.9.1 and 2.10 and is still present on nightly and main. It only affects the fp8-**output** path with a non-trivial output scale; bf16/fp16/fp32 output (where `scale_result` is documented as not applied) is unaffected.

Source references (main):
- https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/aten/src/ATen/native/cuda/ScaledBlas.cpp#L518-L519 - `scale_result` validated, never used.
- https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/aten/src/ATen/native/cuda/ScaledBlas.cpp#L665 - `_scaled_gemm(...)` called with no `scale_result`.
- https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/aten/src/ATen/native/cuda/ScaledBlas.cpp#L388 - result-scale slot hardwired to `std::nullopt`.
- https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/aten/src/ATen/cuda/CUDABlas.cpp#L1874-L1876 - `CUBLASLT_MATMUL_DESC_D_SCALE_POINTER` set only when the pointer is non-null.

Minimal self-contained repro:

```python
import torch

E4M3 = torch.float8_e4m3fn
dev = "cuda"
torch.manual_seed(0)
M, K, N = 32, 16, 32

# Integer operands in {-1,0,1}, K=16 -> accumulated sums in [-16,16], all exact
# in e4m3; 0.5*ref lands on the 0.5 grid (also exact). Any nonzero diff is a real
# bug, not fp reordering.
a_int = torch.randint(-1, 2, (M, K), device=dev)
b_int = torch.randint(-1, 2, (K, N), device=dev)
A = a_int.float().to(E4M3)
B = b_int.float().t().contiguous().t().to(E4M3)  # column-major B

one = torch.tensor(1.0, device=dev)
half = torch.tensor(0.5, device=dev)

o1 = torch._scaled_mm(A, B, scale_a=one, scale_b=one, scale_result=one, out_dtype=E4M3)
o2 = torch._scaled_mm(A, B, scale_a=one, scale_b=one, scale_result=half, out_dtype=E4M3)

ref = (A.float().double() @ B.float().double()).cpu()
o1_eq_o2 = bool(torch.equal(o1.view(torch.int8), o2.view(torch.int8)))
err2 = float((o2.float().double().cpu() - 0.5 * ref).abs().max())

print("torch", torch.__version__)
print("o1 == o2 bitwise:", o1_eq_o2, "(expected False; True == bug)")
print("err deq(o2) vs 0.5*ref:", err2, "(expected 0.0; nonzero == scale_result ignored)")
```

Observed vs expected:

```
Expected (scale_result honored): o1 != o2 bitwise, and deq(o2) == 0.5*ref exactly.

torch 2.9.1+cu128       -> o1 == o2 bitwise: False   err deq(o2) vs 0.5*ref: 0.0   (correct)
torch 2.12.0.dev20260408+cu128 -> o1 == o2 bitwise: True    err deq(o2) vs 0.5*ref: 4.5   (BUG)

On nightly/main o1 == o2 and deq(o2) == ref: the requested output scale of 0.5 had
zero effect. The err=0.0 pass on 2.9.1 also validates the multiply-convention
direction of the oracle, ruling out a repro bug.
```

Related issues: #119135 ("`scale_result` not changing result tensor at all", Feb 2024) is CLOSED (fixed 2024-04-23); 2.9.1 still honors `scale_result`, so the current 2.10+/main behavior is a re-regression of that path introduced by the `Blas.cpp` -> `ScaledBlas.cpp` refactor. #154807 ("FP8 scaled mm lowering ignores scale_result argument", open) is the Inductor-lowering manifestation and is plausibly the same root cause surfacing at the compile layer. Original report for reference: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/119135 ("In `_scaled_mm`, `scale_result` not changing result tensor at all", Feb 2024) reports the same symptom historically. Note that 2.9.1 in this audit does honor `scale_result`, so the current nightly/main behavior is a re-regression of that path rather than the original report; it may or may not be tracked by the same issue.

### Versions

Note: the collect_env below is the stable pod (torch 2.9.1+cu128). The nightly venv on the same H100 pod is torch 2.12.0.dev20260408+cu128 (same CUDA 12.8, driver 580.126.09, cuDNN, and H100 hardware).


Affected: torch 2.12.0.dev20260408+cu128 (nightly, confirmed by run), and release/2.10 and main (per source inspection: same `_scaled_gemm` signature drop). Not affected: torch 2.9.1+cu128 (confirmed by run, `scale_result` honored). Regression window: introduced by the `Blas.cpp` -> `ScaledBlas.cpp` refactor between 2.9.1 and 2.10.

instruction: Analyze this software issue and explain the possible cause and solution.

input.title: CMake/Build Changes for WoA CTK and RTX Spark
input.description:
## Motivation

Windows Arm64 CUDA builds currently fail or require local patching because PyTorch’s build logic assumes Windows CUDA Toolkit libraries live under `lib/x64`.

## Summary

This PR adds Windows Arm64 CUDA Toolkit library layout support to PyTorch’s CUDA discovery and CUDA extension build paths (used by audio/vision etc).

The Windows Arm64 CUDA Toolkit uses architecture-specific library directories such as `lib/arm64`, while PyTorch currently assumes the existing Windows x64 layout, primarily `lib/x64`. This breaks CUDA Toolkit library discovery during PyTorch configuration and downstream CUDA extension builds against an installed PyTorch wheel.

This PR updates:

- PyTorch’s bundled `FindCUDAToolkit.cmake` to search `lib/arm64` and `lib/arm64/stubs` on Windows Arm64.
- PyTorch’s bundled legacy `FindCUDA.cmake` fallback to include `lib/arm64` for completeness.
- `torch.utils.cpp_extension` so `CUDAExtension`, JIT extensions, torchvision, torchaudio, and other extension builds use `CUDA_HOME/lib/arm64` on native Windows Arm64.
- `torch.utils.cpp_extension` Visual Studio environment selection to recognize `win-arm64`.
- CUDA extension arch parsing for `10.3f`, `12.0f` and `12.1f`.

References:

- Windows Arm64 CUDA Toolkit Dev Preview: [WoA CTK Download](https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda-13-4-0-download-archive?target_os=Windows&target_arch=arm64)

## Details

For CMake discovery, the new Arm64 suffixes are prepended only when:

```cmake
WIN32 AND CMAKE_SYSTEM_PROCESSOR STREQUAL "ARM64"
```

## Testing

Validated locally with a Windows Arm64 PyTorch CUDA wheel build using the new CUDA Toolkit layout.

Also validated that CUDA extension library paths resolve to the Arm64 CUDA Toolkit directory when building against the resulting torch installation.

Existing Windows x64 behavior remains unchanged because `lib/x64` remains the default unless the Python platform and/or CMAKE_SYSTEM_PROCESSOR is Arm64.

input.title: [distributed] Generalize logical RNG replay and add uniform support
input.description:
Stack from [ghstack](https://github.com/ezyang/ghstack/tree/0.15.0) (oldest at bottom):
* #189546
* #190445
* __->__ #190444
* #190289

Replace the parent normal-specific flat-slice backend with one private distribution-kind operator sharing logical layout validation and dense Philox reservation. Preserve the normal bitstream exactly and add uniform as kind 1 with the legacy CUDA transform and bounds semantics.

Use a Python op-spec table and one Meta implementation so another fixed-consumption scalar distribution needs only a spec entry, a CUDA transform case, and tests. Validate kind, parameter arity and type, distribution arguments, and logical layouts before generator reservation.

Keep the reusable normal_/uniform_ adapter, including explicit-generator rankwise replay for LocalTensor.

Test Plan:
- ninja -C build install -j 96
- python test/distributed/test_stateful_rng.py
- lintrunner on the touched ATen, Meta, adapter, and test files

input.title: [random] Add logical-index stateless Philox generation
input.description:
Stack from [ghstack](https://github.com/ezyang/ghstack/tree/0.15.0) (oldest at bottom):
* #190447
* #190446
* #189546
* #190445
* #190444
* __->__ #190443
* #190289

Add private indexed uniform and normal operators that map compact block descriptors to positions in one logical stateless Philox draw. Preserve the existing dense APIs, validate layouts in native and Meta paths, and provide compile coverage.

Document the follow-up architecture for sharing logical RNG layouts with the legacy Generator adapter and DTensor without changing existing stateful streams.

Test Plan:
- ninja -C build install
- python test/test_stateless_rng.py
- python test/distributed/test_stateful_rng.py
- python test/test_public_bindings.py -k test_no_new_bindings
- python test/test_public_bindings.py -k test_correct_module_names
